Keywords: frequency animation, when to animate, repeat action animation
Why It Matters: 100+ daily actions feel slow when animated. Raycast has zero open/close animation because it's invoked hundreds of times a day
Do: Use the frequency table: 100+/day = no animation, tens/day = drastically reduce, occasional = standard, rare = can delight
Don't: Animate every state change because it 'looks polished'

Keywords: keyboard shortcut, keyboard action, command palette, hotkey
Why It Matters: Keyboard actions are repeated hundreds of times daily; animation makes the interface feel slow, delayed, and disconnected from the user's intent
Do: Skip animation entirely for keyboard-triggered open/close/submit
Don't: Apply enter animations to elements opened by keyboard shortcut
Code Good: instant: open command palette with display: none → block

Principle: Every animation needs a clear purpose
Keywords: why animate, animation purpose, decorative animation
Why It Matters: If the only reason is 'looks cool' and the user sees it often, the animation is friction, not polish
Do: Audit each animation against: spatial consistency, state indication, explanation, feedback, or preventing jarring change
Don't: Add motion as decoration without a purpose
Code Good: Toast slides in from same direction it can be swiped away
Code Bad: Random parallax on every section just to fill space

Principle: Use ease-out for entries and exits
Keywords: ease-out, easing curve, enter animation, exit animation
Why It Matters: ease-out starts fast and decelerates — the user sees movement immediately at the moment they're watching most closely
Do: Default to ease-out for any element entering or leaving the screen
Don't: Use ease-in on UI (it delays the first frame, feels sluggish)

Keywords: custom easing, cubic-bezier, strong easing
Why It Matters: Built-in ease/ease-out/ease-in-out are too weak. They lack the punch that makes animations feel intentional
Do: Use the curve set: --ease-out: cubic-bezier(0.23, 1, 0.32, 1); --ease-in-out: cubic-bezier(0.77, 0, 0.175, 1); --ease-drawer: cubic-bezier(0.32, 0.72, 0, 1)
Don't: Ship with the default keyword 'ease-out' on a hero animation

Keywords: ease-in-out, on-screen motion, morph
Why It Matters: Elements moving across the screen (not entering/exiting) need both acceleration and deceleration to feel natural
Do: Use ease-in-out cubic-bezier(0.77, 0, 0.175, 1) for elements morphing or repositioning while visible
Don't: Apply ease-out to a layout shift between two visible states
Code Good: cubic-bezier(0.77, 0, 0.175, 1) on a tab indicator slide
Code Bad: ease-out on a tab indicator that slides between visible tabs

Keywords: linear easing, marquee, progress bar
Why It Matters: Marquees, progress bars, and infinite spinners need constant velocity — easing distorts a continuous loop
Do: Use linear for marquee, progress, infinite spin
Don't: Apply ease-out to a CSS marquee

Keywords: duration, timing, animation length
Why It Matters: A 180ms dropdown feels more responsive than a 400ms one. Past 300ms users start to feel they're waiting
Do: Reference the table: button press 100-160ms, tooltip 125-200ms, dropdown 150-250ms, modal/drawer 200-500ms
Don't: Default to 300-500ms because it 'feels smoother'

Keywords: asymmetric timing, enter exit duration, slow press fast release
Why It Matters: Press should be deliberate (slow); release should be system response (fast). The system is fast; the user is deciding
Do: Press: 1-2s linear for hold-to-delete. Release: 200ms ease-out always
Don't: Use the same 300ms ease for both press and release

Principle: Use springs for drag and gesture
Keywords: spring animation, drag gesture, momentum
Why It Matters: Springs simulate real physics; they maintain velocity when interrupted while CSS keyframes restart from zero
Do: Use Motion's useSpring for drag interactions, mouse-tracking decoration, and Apple-style 'alive' elements like Dynamic Island
Don't: Use a fixed-duration tween for gesture-driven motion

Principle: Apple-style spring config is easier to reason about
Keywords: spring config, bounce, apple spring
Why It Matters: Duration + bounce maps to human intuition; mass/stiffness/damping requires physics
Do: Prefer { type: spring, duration: 0.5, bounce: 0.2 } over raw mass/stiffness/damping
Don't: Mix spring and tween in the same component
Code Bad: { type: 'spring', mass: 1, stiffness: 100, damping: 10 } (when defaults work)

Principle: Keep bounce subtle in product UI
Keywords: bounce, spring overshoot
Why It Matters: Heavy bounce reads as toy-like; only acceptable for drag-to-dismiss or playful brand moments
Do: Bounce: 0.1-0.3 in product UI; 0 for dashboards; up to 0.5 for playful brand work
Don't: Use bounce 0.5+ on a settings dialog
Code Bad: bounce: 0.6 on a settings panel slide-in

Keywords: button press, active state, scale 0.97, button feedback
Why It Matters: Without :active feedback the button feels dead. Scale 0.97 confirms the UI heard the user
Do: Add transform: scale(0.97) on :active with transition 160ms ease-out
Don't: Ship a button without :active state
Code Good: button:active { transform: scale(0.97) } and transition: transform 160ms ease-out

Keywords: scale 0 entry, popping in, scale entry
Why It Matters: Nothing in the real world appears from nothing. scale(0) reads as artificial
Do: Start from scale(0.9-0.95) combined with opacity: 0
Don't: Animate entry from transform: scale(0)

Keywords: popover transform-origin, popover anchor, radix popover
Why It Matters: Center-origin popovers feel disconnected from the trigger. Origin-aware popovers feel like they grew from where you clicked
Do: Use transform-origin: var(--radix-popover-content-transform-origin) (Radix) or var(--transform-origin) (Base UI). Modals are exempt — keep transform-origin: center
Don't: Apply transform-origin: center to a popover anchored to a button
Code Bad: transform-origin: center on a Radix Popover content

Principle: Skip delay on subsequent tooltip hovers
Keywords: tooltip delay, instant tooltip, toolbar hover
Why It Matters: Initial delay prevents accidental hover triggers. Once one tooltip is open, the user has clearly committed; instant subsequent tooltips make the toolbar feel fast
Do: Use data-instant attribute to set transition-duration: 0ms on tooltips after the first
Don't: Apply the same enter delay to every tooltip hover

Principle: Use blur to mask imperfect crossfades
Keywords: blur transition, crossfade blur, button state blur
Why It Matters: Without blur you see two distinct states overlapping. Blur bridges the visual gap by blending them into a single perceived transformation
Do: Add filter: blur(2px) during state transitions; keep under 20px (Safari paints heavy blur slowly)
Don't: Crossfade two button-content states with no blur and identical timing
Code Bad: crossfade with no blur, two states visible together

Keywords: @starting-style, css entry animation, no useEffect
Why It Matters: Modern CSS replaces the React useEffect mounted=true pattern. Browser-native entry without JavaScript
Do: Wrap entry state in @starting-style { ... } block; fall back to data-mounted attribute for older browsers
Don't: Default to JS-mounted state for every component entry

Principle: Prefer CSS transitions over keyframes for dynamic UI
Keywords: transition vs keyframe, interruptible animation, dynamic ui
Why It Matters: Transitions can be interrupted and retargeted mid-animation. Keyframes restart from zero — toasts added rapidly look broken
Do: Use transition for any state that can be triggered rapidly (toasts, list adds, hover toggles). Reserve keyframes for one-shot reveals
Don't: Use @keyframes for a toast slide-in
Code Bad: @keyframes slideIn used on a Sonner-style toast

Principle: Use translateY/X percentages for size-agnostic motion
Keywords: translateY 100%, percentage transform, drawer hidden
Why It Matters: Percentage values in translate() are relative to the element's own size. The drawer hides correctly regardless of its height
Do: Use translateY(100%) to hide a drawer by exactly its height
Don't: Hardcode pixel offsets that break on different content lengths
Code Good: transform: translateY(100%) hides drawer regardless of height
Code Bad: transform: translateY(420px) on a variable-height drawer

Keywords: transform opacity only, gpu animation, layout thrash
Why It Matters: transform and opacity skip layout and paint, running on the GPU. Animating width/height/margin/padding triggers all three rendering steps
Do: Always animate transform (translate, scale, rotate) and opacity for visible motion
Don't: Animate width, height, margin, or padding on a fast interaction

Principle: Don't animate CSS variables on parents with many children
Keywords: css variable performance, swipe amount drawer, recalc
Why It Matters: Updating a CSS var on a parent recalculates styles for every child — expensive for drawers with many items
Do: Set transform directly on the dragging element, not via a CSS variable on its container
Don't: Use --swipe-amount on a drawer container to drive child positions during drag

Principle: Framer Motion x/y shorthand is NOT hardware-accelerated
Keywords: framer motion performance, motion x y, hardware acceleration
Why It Matters: Motion's shorthand props use requestAnimationFrame on the main thread. Under load they drop frames. The full transform string runs on the GPU
Do: Use animate={{ transform: 'translateX(100px)' }} for any animation that runs while the page is also loading
Don't: Use animate={{ x: 100 }} for animations that play during page load or heavy script work

Principle: CSS animations beat JS under load
Keywords: css vs js animation, main thread, dropped frames
Why It Matters: CSS animations run off the main thread. While the browser is busy loading or scripting, JS-driven animations drop frames; CSS stays smooth
Do: Use CSS for predetermined animations. Reserve JS for dynamic, interruptible ones
Don't: Drive a logo scale-in with Framer Motion that plays during page load
Code Bad: Framer Motion shared-layout animation during route change

Principle: Use WAAPI for programmatic CSS animations
Keywords: web animations api, waapi, element.animate
Why It Matters: Web Animations API gives JS control with CSS performance — hardware-accelerated, interruptible, no library
Do: Use element.animate([{...}, {...}], { duration, easing, fill }) when you need JS to trigger CSS-grade motion
Don't: Reach for Framer Motion to play a single one-off clip-path animation
Code Bad: Framer Motion installed for a single clip-path tween

Keywords: velocity dismiss, swipe flick, momentum
Why It Matters: Quick flicks should dismiss regardless of distance. Pure threshold dismissal feels sticky
Do: Calculate velocity = dragDistance / elapsed; dismiss when velocity > 0.11 OR distance > threshold
Don't: Require dragging past a fixed pixel threshold to dismiss

Principle: Apply damping at boundaries instead of hard stops
Keywords: drag boundary, damping, friction edge
Why It Matters: Real-world things slow before stopping. Hard stops feel like the UI broke
Do: Allow over-drag with rubber-band damping (each pixel of drag yields less movement past the boundary)
Don't: Lock the drag at the boundary with no give
Code Good: reduce dragDelta by factor 0.4 once past max position
Code Bad: clamp(drag, min, max) with abrupt cutoff at boundaries

Principle: Capture pointer and ignore secondary touches
Keywords: pointer capture, multi-touch protection, drag continuation
Why It Matters: Without pointer capture the drag stops if the pointer leaves the element. Without multi-touch protection a finger swap mid-drag jumps the element
Do: setPointerCapture on dragstart; ignore subsequent touches while isDragging is true
Don't: Listen on document for mouseup without pointer capture; allow new touches mid-drag
Code Good: element.setPointerCapture(e.pointerId); if (isDragging) return on new press

Keywords: prefers-reduced-motion, reduced motion, motion sickness
Why It Matters: Reduced motion means fewer and gentler animations — not zero. Keep opacity and color transitions that aid comprehension; remove translate/scale
Do: Wrap motion-based transforms in @media (prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference); ship a reduced fallback that uses opacity only
Don't: Disable all transitions when prefers-reduced-motion is set, or ignore the query entirely
Code Good: @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { .x { animation: fade 0.2s ease } /* no transform */ }
Code Bad: ignore prefers-reduced-motion or { animation: none } for everything

Principle: Gate hover animations behind hover/pointer media query
Keywords: touch hover, hover media query, false hover
Why It Matters: Touch devices fire :hover on tap. Hover-driven scale animations cause visual jumps when users tap
Do: Wrap hover styles in @media (hover: hover) and (pointer: fine) { ... }
Don't: Apply :hover transform: scale(1.05) without media gating

Keywords: stagger, list animation, cascade
Why It Matters: All-at-once entries feel busy. Long staggers feel slow. 30-80ms produces a satisfying cascade without delaying interaction
Do: Use animation-delay nth-child or Motion's staggerChildren in 30-80ms steps; never block interaction during stagger
Don't: Stagger 200ms+ between every list item or animate everything at once
Code Bad: every list item delayed 250ms or all at delay 0

Principle: Cohesion: motion personality must match component personality
Keywords: animation personality, motion mood, brand cohesion
Why It Matters: Sonner uses 'ease' and slightly slower timing because it fits its elegant brand. A dashboard would use crisp ease-out and fast timings
Do: Audit motion against the rest of the design: brand voice, copy tone, component personality should all align
Don't: Apply the same 200ms ease-out to a playful animated mascot AND a banking dashboard
Code Good: Sonner's slower 'ease' on a casual toast
Code Bad: 250ms ease-out on the same toast in a strict bank app

Keywords: animation review, fresh eyes, slow motion testing
Why It Matters: Fresh eyes catch imperfections invisible during development. Slow motion playback exposes timing issues at 1x
Do: Sleep on it. The next day, play animations at 0.25x in DevTools. Check colors don't show two states overlapping, easing doesn't stop abruptly
Don't: Ship the animation the same day you wrote it
Code Bad: approve at 1x speed end of day

Principle: Test gestures on real devices not simulators
Keywords: real device testing, gesture testing, mobile drawer
Why It Matters: Touch interactions, drag gestures, and momentum behave differently on real hardware. Simulators miss latency and friction effects
Do: Connect phone via USB, visit local dev server by IP, use Safari/Chrome remote devtools
Don't: Approve drawer drag behavior using only the iOS Simulator
Code Good: real iPhone via Safari Web Inspector
Code Bad: Xcode Simulator only for drag-to-dismiss QA

Keywords: library defaults, sane defaults, dx
Why It Matters: Most users never customize. Default easing, timing, and visual design must be excellent — that determines adoption
Do: Ship beautiful out of the box; expose options as escape hatches not requirements
Don't: Force the user to pick a duration and easing on every toast call

Keywords: edge case polish, invisible details, tab visibility
Why It Matters: Pause toast timers when the tab hides. Fill stack gaps with pseudo-elements to keep hover state. Capture pointer during drag. Users never notice — that's the point
Do: Pause animation timers on visibilitychange; use ::before/::after to bridge gaps in stacked components
Don't: Let toast timers run while tab is hidden, expiring before user returns
Code Bad: toast.dismiss after 4s real-time even if tab was hidden

Keywords: devtools animations, frame by frame, animation inspector
Why It Matters: Frame-by-frame inspection reveals timing issues between coordinated properties (color + transform + opacity) that you cannot see at 1x
Do: Open Chrome DevTools → Animations panel; reduce playback to 25%; step through frame-by-frame
Don't: Eyeball animations at full speed and call it done
